sentences.text: Good morning, Mr.
Ferree.
Good morning, Ms.
Walters.
Charles, C-H-A-R-L-E-S.
Victor, just like Victor.
Ferree, F-E-R-R-E-E.
Same.
June the 30th, 1924.
Prescott, Arizona.
Sure.
My mother's maiden name was Contreras.
Her ancestors were from Spain and Mexico, and she-- her nationality was Spanish, Mexican, and Indian.
Her family were ranchers in the Miller Valley in Arizona.
They had a huge, big cattle ranch.
Seven or eight brothers and sisters.
My father was a railroad man.
He worked for the Southern Pacific Railroad and Santa Fe Railroad.
His name was Ben, Ben Ferree.
Not, Benjamin-- Ben.
And he, he worked as a freight-- on freight trains as a brakeman and passenger trains as a conductor, and also as a fireman, stoking coal into the engines.
I was raised in Prescott.
I went to Catholic school starting at about age 3.
And it was not an orphanage, but it did have children in there that were-- that had no parents or were very poor.
My education started in this Catholic academy, they called it.
So, by the time I was five, I could read and write as good as most kids that were seven or eight years old.
We moved-- my family moved from Prescott to Tucson in 1929, in the middle of the Depression.
Things were very tight.
My dad worked at a variety of jobs.
We-- my brother and I were farmed out to various families temporarily.
We were never-- we always knew our parents were there, but it was kind of a very awkward situation, because we didn't get to see them all the time.
That lasted for three or four years.
And we were also placed in the Catholic orphanage at one time during the height of the Depression.
And those things were kind of traumatic for both of us.
My brother was a very small, skinny, sickly boy, and I was a healthy, much bigger person-- child.
His name was Benjamin Francis.
He was born January the 21st, 1926.
His-- Benjamin Francis.
He has since passed away, about 15 years ago.
When I started school in Government Heights in Tucson-- I never will forget this.
My first teacher, whose name was Miss Ressor, R-E-S-S-O-R.
And she-- I had her for two grades.
I have pictures of her somewhere around here.
And I-- she was just a wonderful woman.
I met her after the war.
We kept in touch until I was probably 22, 23, when I left Arizona to come to California, go to California.
Oh, it-- Catholic religion-- in the Catholic academy, is what they called it.
It's since been dis-- replaced by a school.
But it was called the Saint something-- St.
Joseph's Academy, I believe.
And we were taught by the nuns to read, and write, and draw-- kind of like kindergarten, I would suspect.
Not at that age.
When I-- when we moved to Tucson, I, I-- my mother was a very strict Catholic.
My father was not Catholic.
He was-- I don't know what he was.
Not a very religious person.
But my mother was, and we-- my brother and I were both altar boys for Father Stoner.
And the reason that I remember him so dramatically is because he had two full-size dachshunds, dachshunds-- not little ones, big ones.
And one of our duties as altar boys was to take his dachs-- his dogs for a walk.
I don't remember their names, but these dogs were big enough-- two of them on the same leash.
You had-- you were dragged all over the neighborhood because you couldn't-- the dogs were very powerful.
Father Stoner was a very strict and businesslike man.
He's-- I don't recall having any real friendly, warm feelings toward him.
He insisted on his altar boys being-- learning precisely the way he wanted them to say mass, the way his-- their mannerisms and so on.
So I don't have fond recollections of that part of my religious training.
I learned Latin through him-- some Latin, of course-- since that's how they said mass in those days.
And I began, when I was in my early teens, to question some, some of the Catholic dogma, and I kind of just disappeared.
Over my mother's objections, I kind of just dropped out of the Church for several years.
Oh, I was-- I was interested in sports, mostly.
I was very athletic.
My brother was, too, although he was much smaller than me.
He was a terrific swimmer, and I was, too.
And one of the benefits of living in Tucson, which was very hot, was that we, in the summertime, spent most of the time in the swimming pools.
And they had numerous public pools that-- very inexpensive.
My brother became a real champion diver and a very fast swimmer. 18 months younger than me, but-- and I was much bigger and stronger than him.
But he could swim, and he was fearless diving off of 60-foot platforms into the water.
And I would jump.
I jumped many times from that platform before I had the courage to dive off.
But Ben, my Benny-- I called him Benny-- he just wasn't afraid.
So he was really-- he was not big enough to play football.
I was playing tackle football by the time I was in the seventh grade in the schools-- public schools.

Incidentally, I might add that because of my early Catholic education-- if you could call it that-- in the-- in Prescott, at the academy-- when I was in elementary school in Miss Ressor's class, in the first grade, I ended up being double promoted from end of the second-- to the second grade-- regular promotion.
Skipped the third grade, because I was too far ahead of the children in that class.
And then I was double promoted again, and I skipped the fourth grade.
So I ended up in the fifth grade when, ordinarily, I would have been in the third grade.
So I was actually two years ahead of other kids my age.
But I was big for my age so it didn't-- that was not a problem, except for the kids that knew that I was younger than they were.
So, tell me about your high school and what happened after high school.
High school was-- well, junior high school was even more fun, in some ways, than high school for me.
Because I went from the sixth grade to the seventh grade to a school where-- with children who I didn't know.
Kids from all over town went to the junior high school, which was seventh, eighth, and ninth.
That's when I became interested-- pretty-- I always was interested in pretty girls, even in the first grade.
But by the time I got to Safford Junior High School, I was-- I was very aware of, of, of girls and always had a girlfriend.
A lot of good memories of those days.
But the big thing I remember about Safford Junior High School was that I was-- in addition to being a good student, I was a good athlete.

So I got to play first string tackle football with the junior high school team from the seventh grade through the ninth grade, and-- which meant playing against some real good football players from other towns.
And really, I enjoyed that very much.
And, and it's true what they say about football-- does teach you some values.
It taught me some values-- sportsmanship, how to control your temper, things like that.
I got-- When I went to high school, I was still two years ahead of-- two years younger than most of my class.
Although there were other students in the high school-- which was the 10th grade-- who were my age or maybe just a year older than me.
Or a year-- yeah, a year older than me.
And I also went out for football and made the team, and I got to play first string football all three years in high school.
And that-- and, and and that was really the highlight.
I was a real good student until I started into, into algebra.
And for some reason, I just didn't get algebra.
The coach persuaded the algebra teacher to pass me one more time, even though I didn't have a clue, so I could finish out my senior year and football.
I beg your pardon?
Theater, music.
I was-- I was-- I had-- I was a musician in terms of learning-- playing in the bands.
Of course, I couldn't play at football games, but I liked to play in the band.
I also was-- I played the trumpet and the drums.
I also played in the-- in school in the dance bands for proms and stuff.
I was in the American Legion drum and bugle corps, which was where I started, actually-- my interest in music.
I was a-- I was a basso at the age of about 13.
And I mean a basso.
The music-- the voice teacher, whose name was Arlene [?
Seletti-- ?] I'll never forget her as long as I live.
Beautiful woman, and I had a real crush on her.
She-- she tutored me, more or less.
And therefore, I got to sing solos at the school functions.
And I can remember the song that she liked me to sing was 'Water Boy' because you-- it required a bass-- a bass voice.
I beg your pardon?
Oh, no.
Oh, I can't reach those notes anymore.
Oh, let's see.
Yeah, but I can't go that low.
It's about this black man working on the-- on th-- on the railroad with a sledgehammer.
And the song is-- let's see, I don't know.
This is going to be bad.
[SINGING] Water boy, where are you hiding?
If you don't-a come, gonna climb this mountain.
Goes on and on about the sledgehammer.
And he's calling for water for he and his coworkers.
Kind of like a chain gang song.
After high school.
In the meantime, during high school, as with most of my friends, we had joined the National Guard.
Because we got $5 a month to go to National Guard training.
Yeah, during-- well, we had ROTC in high school, and we lied about our age.
They didn't care.
I was 15 and was in the National Guard.
And they're with men, grown men.
But it didn't matter, because our, our commanding officer was also our, ROTC-- RO-- Reserve Officer Training teacher in high school.
His name was Captain Mitchell, and-- Michaels.
I beg your pardon, Captain Michaels.
And he was our company commander in the National Guard.
The rea-- the motivation was not so much patriotism because the war hadn't even started yet.
It was the $5 a month you got to go to the National Guard once a month.
In 1960-- in 1940, President Roosevelt mobilized the National Guards, all the National Guards, all 48 states.
And I-- the Arizona National Guard became part of the 45th Division.
I had an option, since I was only 16 at the time-- in September of 1940-- to resign, to tell them the truth.
And I could have avoided being called in, being, being activated.
But since so many of my friends were, and I was young enough, I thought, I'll go ahead and do my year, and it'll be good for me.
And come back and finish my ed-- I had graduated from high school.
My intentions were to work for a year and then try to go to college at the University of Arizona.
I had-- my mother and I had kind of decided that I would-- should be a doctor.
That was my mama's wish.
I didn't feel that strongly about it, but I thought, well, I'll-- I want to go to college if I can.
And why not take, take pre-med, which was available at the university.
OK, in September of 1940, the National Guard was activated with Captain Michaels, as I mentioned-- my high school teacher, ROTC teacher, was our company commander.
I could have gone to him and said-- you know, and he knew that I wasn't 18.
But I didn't, and so I went with the rest of the 158th Infantry Regiment, which was the Tucson regiment, to Fort Sill, Oklahoma, as part of the--
Fort Sill, S-I-L-L.
This was an old Indian fighting post in Lawton, Oklahoma.
Been there for many, many, many years.
It was an old army fort.
A fort and a camp-- in military terms, a fort is a permanent installation; a camp is a temporary installation.
Because I was in camps and forts.
Fort Sill was a permanent installation with regular army troops stationed there that had been for years.
We were in Fort Sill.
We took our basic training in the infantry.
Didn't have weapons, didn't have proper uniforms.
They weren't ready for us.
We just lived in tents for a year.
Rain, mud, you name it.
It was cold and miserable in that-- in the wintertime, and hotter than blazes in the summertime.
But eventually, we were issued old Enfield and Springfield rifles, some of which were English weapons, as a matter of fact.
We went to Abilene, Texas, to Camp Barkeley in about spring of 1941.
And that was-- we were the first military troops to be in Camp Barkeley since the end of the Indian wars.
This was a temporary camp designed to train two or three divisions at a time.
The 45th Division was the first one that went there.
In June--
It was infan-- we were being trained to-- com-- for combat as infantry soldiers.
And I had several jobs.
I was the-- I was the heavy weapons operator with a .50 caliber machine gun, which was the biggest machine gun.
I was in the mortar platoon with the 81 millimeter mortars, which was a bad job.
I hated that job.
I played in the regimental band with a bugle, and I got to be a bugler temporarily.
But that-- another other guy who was better than me won out that job.
That was a cushy job, to be the bugler.
I was temporarily assigned to a machine gun squad, and I was a, a gunner and a-- and a driver of a 3/4 ton truck.
These were when they were getting to be mobile as opposed to walking.
That didn't last very long.
Most people have never heard of the big maneuvers of 1941 in Louisiana.
We-- this was the biggest, up to that point in time, military maneuvers ever held in this country by the United States Army.
And there were literally, by then, millions of soldiers, aviators involved in the maneuvers.
And that was really a test.
If you could handle that in the summertime, in the jungles, it-- which is where-- we thought, you know, we were going to end up in the South Pacific.
And this is still 1941.
The maneuvers were still going on in September.
But I-- my year-- I had a one-year contract with the Army of United States, and they had to let me out.
Had I been drafted, it was for the duration.
So, in the-- I was kind of like the cadre, which I mentioned.
A cadre is-- is-- is-- is kind of like the skeleton of a-- of an organization, and the bo-- the meat is the draftees.
So the cadre comes in, the cadre forms the foundation of the organization, and the draftees come in and flesh it out under the training of the cadre men.
And I was a cadre person.
OK that-- in 1941, in September, they had to release me and several other of my friends, because they had signed up for one year.
Well, that was a mistake.
They couldn't get away-- couldn't get--
In 19-- well, the ar-- the National Guard ceased to exist in 1940.
It became the Army of the United States.
So, there was no more National Guard.
So, when I-- when we were activated, we became the Army of the United States.
No more National Guard.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Some people still referred to it, but it wasn't the National Guard.
It was the regular Army.
The distinction is, the United States Army is the regular army.
The Army of United States is what they created with the draft and the mobilization of the National Guard.
So you have the-- it's a very fine distinction.
It doesn't really matter.
In September of 1941, I was discharged and went back to Tucson.
I went back to school, and I was still only 17 years old.
I started at the University of Arizona.
And by then, I had decided that I wanted to be-- having experienced the infantry, and realizing that that is not what I want to do if I have to go to war-- it's just too hard.
It's too hard.
It's too dangerous.
And I was-- I don't want to say smart enough.
I didn't want to do that.
It was just too hard.
It was-- that-- it's-- those infantry guys, hands down, had the worst job of anybody.
They're the ones that had to do the out-front, hand-to-hand fighting and one-on-one.
And that-- casualty rates were fierce.
So I decided to try to get enough education to qualify for the Army Air Corps, which is why I went back to school.
And I was in school 8-- about 18 months, and I took the test.
By then I was 18.
I took the test for the Air-- Army Air Corps.
Passed the test.
Had an option of waiting to be drafted or going in-- volunteering.
And I decided, why wait?
The sooner I get in, the quicker I'm going to get a commission and get to fly.
That was my dream.
So I went back in the service in 1942, late 1942.
I went to camp-- rather, let me start.
I went to Fort MacArthur, California, with a cadre again, receiving draftees, getting them uniforms, haircuts, marching them around.
It's a bunch guys who've never-- you know, it was-- I can remember how they felt, having been there myself.
But we were God to those people.
I was a corporal, and they didn't know that they could-- didn't say sir-- didn't have to say, sir.
They would say-- they'd 'yes, sir' you to death.
They didn't have to do that.
So you'd tell them, you don't have to say sir to me.
Some of them, it didn't matter.
They'd do it anyway, because everybody was pretty nervous about this experience.
And it's quite a traumatic experience to go from a farm to be-- to become a soldier all-- you know, without any-- without any in-between experience.
But I had the advantage of having already been through basic training, so I didn't have to do that again.
So I was called a cadre person at Fort MacArthur, and mainly just kind of helping these people through the process of becoming acclimated to the military.
I went from there to Camp Roberts, which is around San Luis Obispo to what they call a replacement-- a repo depo, a replacement place-- they were-- base.
There were probably 50-- to 100,000 men in the vicinity of Camp Roberts, in tents again.
All these promises of steam-heated barracks never materialized for me.
That was one of the promises they made to us when going to Fort Sill.
And I never slept in a steam-heated barracks until much later on.
It was always a four-man-- a six-man tent.
At Camp Roberts, I was the cadre again.
And then I was assigned to a new camp, Camp Adair in Oregon, Corvallis-- out by Corvallis-- as a cadre man.
And I still had my, my-- had passed the test.
I was waiting to be called into the Air Corps.

I had friends in-- family friends who were in-- had been in the military who were-- in World War I, especially-- who were help-- were writing letters to officers in the Air For-- in the Air Corps, trying to help me get in.
Because by then, they were taking four-year graduates with engineering degrees.
See, I didn't have that.
I had-- I had two years, and none of it was engineering.
So, if you had a four degree-- a four-year degree with engineering, you were-- and you were qualified otherwise, you were first choice.
So I was way down the ladder, but I didn't know this at the time.
I went-- we built the 70th Division, and this was my home division in Oregon here, in 1943.
From-- and we also were involved with two or three other divisions, which does-- doesn't matter-- all of whom went to Europe.
This, this group was trained in Oregon for the stated purpose of fighting in Europe, because this is similar to Europe in terms of the-- definitely, the countryside, and the weather, and so on.
In the interim, I had been to, to flight school at Fort-- at Fort Sill.
I went back to Fort Sill in 1943 for flight training.
That's a--
